title: Dictionary Comprehensions
subtitle: Build dictionaries in one expressive line
learning_objectives: Write a dictionary comprehension using {key: value for item in iterable}
Build a word-length dictionary from a list of strings
Filter a dictionary with an if condition inside a comprehension
Use zip() to pair two lists and build a dictionary from them

sections.heading: Dictionary Comprehension Syntax
sections.content:
Just as list comprehensions create lists, dictionary comprehensions create dictionaries. The syntax is similar but uses curly braces and requires both a key and a value separated by a colon:

{key_expr: value_expr for item in iterable}

You can also add an if condition at the end to keep only certain items.

sections.heading: Iterating Existing Dicts and Using zip()
sections.content:
When you already have a dictionary, you can iterate its .items() method (which yields (key, value) tuples) to transform or filter it in a comprehension.

zip() lets you pair two separate lists together element by element. It returns an iterator of tuples, which you can unpack directly in a comprehension to build a dictionary from two lists.
sections.note: zip() stops when the shorter list runs out, so always make sure your two lists are the same length when building a dictionary this way.

exercises.title: Word Lengths Dictionary
exercises.prompt: Given fruits = ["apple", "fig", "banana", "kiwi"], use a dictionary comprehension to create a variable called word_lengths that maps each fruit name to its character length. Print the result.
exercises.starter_code:
fruits = ["apple", "fig", "banana", "kiwi"]
# Create a variable called word_lengths using a dictionary comprehension
# It should map each fruit name to its character length
# Print word_lengths
hints: The key is the fruit name and the value is len(fruit).
Syntax: {fruit: len(fruit) for fruit in fruits}

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.

exercises.title: Pair Two Lists with zip()
exercises.prompt: You have two lists: countries = ["France", "Japan", "Brazil"] and capitals = ["Paris", "Tokyo", "Brasilia"]. Use zip() inside a dictionary comprehension to create a variable called capital_map that maps each country to its capital. Print it.
exercises.starter_code:
countries = ["France", "Japan", "Brazil"]
capitals = ["Paris", "Tokyo", "Brasilia"]
# Create a variable called capital_map using a dictionary comprehension with zip()
# It should map each country to its capital
# Print capital_map
exercises.solution:
countries = ["France", "Japan", "Brazil"]
capitals = ["Paris", "Tokyo", "Brasilia"]
capital_map = {country: capital for country, capital in zip(countries, capitals)}
print(capital_map)
hints: Use zip(countries, capitals) to pair them up.
Unpack each pair as: for country, capital in zip(...)

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.

quiz.question: What does {k: v for k, v in d.items() if v > 0} do when applied to a dictionary d?
options: It removes all keys from d that have negative values in place
It creates a new dictionary containing only the key-value pairs where the value is greater than 0
It raises a ValueError if any value is negative
It creates a list of keys whose values are positive
quiz.explanation: The comprehension iterates over d.items() and the if clause filters out pairs where v is not greater than 0, producing a brand new dictionary.

quiz.question: What does zip([1, 2, 3], ["a", "b", "c"]) produce when iterated?
quiz.explanation: zip() pairs elements at the same position together, producing tuples (1, 'a'), (2, 'b'), (3, 'c').

completion_message: Superb! Dictionary comprehensions make data transformation elegant. Combined with zip(), you can build lookup tables from two lists in a single readable line.
